model.label: Claude Opus 4.6
model.description: Anthropic • Hybrid reasoning model that pushes the frontier for coding and AI agents, featuring a 1M context window.

pick.rationale: Florida's elite defense—ranked 5th nationally with an AdjD of 91.8—is the decisive factor in this SEC rematch, as it should contain Vanderbilt's 6th-ranked offense better than the SEC Tournament semifinal where the Commodores shot 55% from the field and 48% from three in a 91-74 blowout. The Gators hold a roughly 5.7-point adjusted efficiency margin advantage (+33.83 vs +28.12) driven by superiority on both ends of the floor, and their 16-2 SEC regular season record as defending national champions reflects a significantly more consistent body of work than Vanderbilt's 11-7 conference mark. While Vanderbilt's recent dominant win and elite offensive firepower led by Tyler Tanner create a legitimate upset threat—especially given the split head-to-head series—Florida's defensive identity, championship experience under Todd Golden, and the likelihood that the SEC Tournament result was an outlier shooting performance make the Gators the clear favorite to advance to the Elite Eight.

reasoningStep.title: South Sweet 16 1: Florida vs Vanderbilt
reasoningStep.summary: Florida's elite defense and roughly 6-point efficiency margin advantage should prevail over Vanderbilt despite the Commodores' recent 91-74 SEC Tournament blowout win, as that result was driven by unsustainable 55% FG and 48% 3PT shooting that Florida's top-5 defense is unlikely to allow again.
evidence: Florida: KenPom #4, AdjEM +33.83, AdjO 125.6 (9th), AdjD 91.8 (top 5), 26-7 record, 16-2 SEC (1st), SRS 27.90 (4th)
Vanderbilt: KenPom #12, AdjEM +28.12, AdjO 127.3 (6th), AdjD 99.2 (28th), 26-8 record, 11-7 SEC (4th), SRS 22.83 (13th)
Head-to-head split: Florida won 98-94 (Jan 17 regular season), Vanderbilt won 91-74 (Mar 14 SEC Tournament semifinal shooting 55% FG, 48% 3PT)
Florida's defense is 7+ points per 100 possessions better than Vanderbilt's (91.8 vs 99.2), creating the primary asymmetry in this matchup
No significant injury concerns identified for either team; both rosters appear healthy

Now I have a comprehensive picture. Let me synthesize all the data:

**Florida (1-seed)**
- KenPom: #4, AdjEM +33.83, AdjO 125.6 (9th), AdjD 91.8 (top 5)
- Torvik: #4, AdjOE 126.1 (11th), AdjDE 92.2 (5th)
- Record: 26-7, 16-2 SEC (1st)
- SRS: 27.90 (4th), SOS: 13.11 (7th)
- Defending national champion

**Vanderbilt (5-seed)**
- KenPom: #12, AdjEM +28.12, AdjO 127.3 (6th), AdjD 99.2 (28th)
- Torvik: #10, AdjOE 127.8 (6th), AdjDE 99.2 (28th)
- Record: 26-8, 11-7 SEC (4th)
- SRS: 22.83 (13th), SOS: 11.68 (23rd)
- Sports-Reference DRtg: 105.4 (145th!) — much worse than KenPom/Torvik suggest

**Head-to-head:**
- Jan 17: Florida 98, Vanderbilt 94 (Florida won at Vanderbilt)
- Mar 14: Vanderbilt 91, Florida 74 (SEC Tournament semifinal — Vanderbilt dominated, shooting 55% FG, 48% from 3)

**Key matchup dynamics:**
- Florida's elite defense (AdjD 91.8) vs Vanderbilt's elite offense (AdjO 127.3)
- Vanderbilt's mediocre defense (AdjD 99.2) vs Florida's strong offense (AdjO 125.6)
- Efficiency margin gap: ~5.7 points per 100 possessions favoring Florida
- The SEC Tournament blowout (91-74) shows Vanderbilt can dominate this matchup when shooting well
- But Florida won the regular season meeting and has the significantly better defense and overall profile

Florida's ~6-point efficiency margin advantage, elite defense, 1-seed status, defending champion pedigree, and stronger overall body of work (16-2 SEC vs 11-7) make them the clear favorite. However, Vanderbilt's recent 17-point blowout win, elite offense, and familiarity with Florida create a credible upset path that warrants moderate confidence rather than high.
